topStories.headline: Golden Gate Bridge Fireworks, Fillmore Jazz Fest Mark Historic July 4th Weekend
topStories.dek: San Francisco celebrates America's 250th birthday with rare fireworks from the Golden Gate Bridge and the West Coast's largest free jazz festival on Fillmore Street — both visible and walkable from Marina and Pacific Heights neighborhoods.
topStories.body:
San Francisco's Fourth of July celebration will be one for the history books this Saturday. To mark the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence, the city will launch fireworks directly from the Golden Gate Bridge — only the third time in the structure's history that pyrotechnics have been fired from the iconic span. The free display is expected to begin at 9:30 p.m. and last approximately 20 minutes. KQED reports that the best viewing locations include Crissy Field, Marina Green, and Pier 39.

Residents of the Marina, Cow Hollow, and Presidio Heights are especially well-positioned for the show. Marina Green and Crissy Field offer wide, grassy viewing areas directly facing the Golden Gate. The SFMTA is adding extra service on several Muni lines, including the 28 19th Avenue and 49 Van Ness/Mission routes. The Golden Gate Bridge will be fully closed to vehicle traffic from 9 p.m. to 10 p.m., and residents should expect increased parking demand throughout nearby neighborhoods.

Meanwhile, the 2026 Fillmore Jazz Festival returns July 4–5 on Fillmore Street between Jackson and Eddy — spanning 12 blocks that cut directly through Pacific Heights. The West Coast's largest free jazz festival draws over 50,000 attendees annually and features more than 25 performers across three main stages named for Tony Bennett, Miles Davis, and John Coltrane. Performances run from 10 a.m. to 6 p.m. both days. SFMTA has announced street closures on Fillmore between Eddy and Jackson beginning at 2 a.m. Saturday through 11:59 p.m. Sunday, with several Muni bus routes rerouted.

Also in the Marina, the annual Fourth of July Pub Crawl returns from 2 to 10 p.m. with check-in at Rick & Roxy's at 2125 Lombard Street. Participating bars include Palm House, Union Street Ale House, White Rabbit, The Blue Light, and more, with drink specials and eating contests at Jaxson. Tickets start at $14.64 via Eventbrite.

topStories.headline: Marina Safeway Tower Faces New Legal Challenge as August Deadline Looms
topStories.dek: Opponents argue the proposed 25-story, 790-unit housing development at 15 Marina Boulevard may not qualify for the state fast-tracking that has kept it on pace for an August 1 design review deadline.
topStories.body:
The contentious proposal to replace the Marina Safeway at 15 Marina Boulevard with a 25-story mixed-use tower continues to generate intense debate as the city's August 1 design review deadline approaches. Developer Align Real Estate's project would include 790 housing units — 86 of them below market rate — along with a replacement Safeway that would be 57% larger than the current store, all designed by the architecture firm Arquitectonica.

As SFist reported in late June, opponents have mounted a new legal challenge arguing the project may not actually qualify for fast-track approval under California's AB 2011 housing law. The state law requires that the majority of surrounding land be developed for "urban uses," and critics contend the Presidio and waterfront open space nearby could disqualify the project. If city officials ultimately determine the project does not meet AB 2011 criteria, Align could face a full environmental review, potentially adding years to the timeline.

The Marina Community Association, led by president Erin Roach, has maintained its position that it supports housing at the site but objects to the building's scale. District 2 Supervisor Stephen Sherrill has called the development "outrageous" and "cartoonish." Meanwhile, housing advocates and YIMBYs have pushed back, with SF YIMBY organizing director Jane Natoli defending the project's compliance with state law. The outcome will have far-reaching implications: Align has three additional Safeway redevelopment projects in the pipeline across San Francisco.

topStories.headline: Presidio Marks 250th Anniversary Year with Exhibits, Food Hall, and New Park Improvements
topStories.dek: The Presidio of San Francisco is celebrating its founding 250 years ago with a slate of exhibitions, archaeological discoveries, and the imminent opening of The Mess Hall food hall at Tunnel Tops.
topStories.body:
The Presidio of San Francisco is in the midst of its 250th anniversary year — commemorating the founding of El Presidio de San Francisco on September 17, 1776, an event that unfolded at the same time the Declaration of Independence was being signed on the East Coast. This week, NBC Bay Area highlighted ongoing archaeological work in the park that has unearthed Spanish colonial pottery and other artifacts. Presidio Trust archaeologist Kari Jones told NBC that the site represents "250 years ago, the beginning of what would become San Francisco."

Visitors can engage with this history through several current exhibitions. "I Am An American: The Nisei Soldier Experience," a traveling exhibit about Japanese American soldiers during World War II, is on display at the Military Intelligence Service Historic Learning Center through August 31. The exhibit is open Monday, Tuesday, and Friday through Sunday from noon to 5 p.m., with $15 admission. The companion exhibition "EXCLUSION," at the Presidio Officers' Club, is free to visit Fridays through Sundays through August. Additionally, through July 4, visitors can earn a limited-edition Anza 250th anniversary Junior Ranger Badge exclusively at the Presidio Visitor Center.

Meanwhile, The Mess Hall, a new 6,200-square-foot food hall adjacent to Presidio Tunnel Tops, has been expected to open this summer. The hall will feature three food concepts — Breadwinner (burgers and sandwiches), Boda (Korean fried chicken and banchan), and Dayboat Seafood — along with a Wrecking Ball Coffee café, a full bar, and a retail market. James Beard Award-winning chef Peter Serpico is leading the culinary development. The venue is located at 201 Halleck Street in a restored former U.S. Army building with sweeping Bay views. An exact opening date has not yet been publicly confirmed, though multiple reports have cited summer 2026 as the target window.

events.title: San Francisco Fourth of July Fireworks — Golden Gate Bridge
events.location: Golden Gate Bridge; best viewed from Crissy Field, Marina Green, and Pier 39
events.description: For only the third time in its history, fireworks will be launched from the Golden Gate Bridge to mark America's 250th birthday. The free show begins at approximately 9:30 p.m. and lasts about 20 minutes. The bridge will be closed to traffic from 9–10 p.m.

events.title: Fillmore Jazz Festival 2026
events.location: Fillmore Street between Jackson and Eddy, Pacific Heights/Fillmore
events.description: The West Coast's largest free jazz festival returns with 25+ artists across three stages named for Tony Bennett, Miles Davis, and John Coltrane. Twelve blocks of Fillmore Street will host live music, food vendors, arts and crafts, and beer gardens.

events.title: Fourth of July Pub Crawl & Hot Dog Eating Contest
events.location: Check-in at Rick & Roxy's, 2125 Lombard Street, Marina District
events.description: The annual Marina District pub crawl visits 10+ bars including Palm House, Union Street Ale House, White Rabbit, and The Blue Light, with drink specials and eating contests at Jaxson. Tickets start at $14.64.

events.title: Presidio Tunnel Tops Campfire Programs
events.location: Campfire Circle, Presidio Tunnel Tops, Presidio
events.description: National Park Service rangers host free daily campfire talks near the Presidio Visitor Center. Open to all ages; no tickets required.

events.title: "I Am An American: The Nisei Soldier Experience" Exhibit
events.location: MIS Historic Learning Center, Building 640, Presidio
events.description: A 1,500-square-foot traveling exhibition honoring Japanese American soldiers who served in WWII. Features firsthand accounts, historical photos, and rare personal artifacts. Admission $15; free for veterans and children under 12.

events.title: Anza 250th Anniversary Junior Ranger Badge
events.location: Presidio Visitor Center, Main Post, Presidio
events.description: For eight days only (June 27–July 4), visitors can earn a limited-edition Anza 250th anniversary Junior Ranger Badge exclusively at the Presidio Visitor Center — a free activity for families.

events.title: BATS Improv: The Short Show & Super Scene
events.location: Bayfront Theatre, Building B, Fort Mason Center, 2 Marina Blvd
events.description: BATS Improv presents two shows: the summertime Short Show (6:45–7:30 p.m.) and Super Scene: Five Stories Start, Only One Ends at 8 p.m. No show on July 4.
events.sourceName: Fort Mason Center for Arts & Culture

events.title: BATS Improv: The Startup — An Improvised Silicon Valley Story
events.location: Bayfront Theatre, Building B, Fort Mason Center, 2 Marina Blvd
events.description: BATS Improv's summer series lampoons the tech world with a fully improvised satirical show set in Silicon Valley. Preceded by The Short Show at 6:45 p.m.
events.sourceName: Fort Mason Center for Arts & Culture

moreNews.headline: Upscale Korean BBQ Restaurant Monami Headed to Upper Fillmore
moreNews.body: Chef Junsoo Bae, the founder of Michelin-starred SSAL on upper Polk, is opening Monami, a new upscale Korean barbecue restaurant at the former Noosh space at 2001 Fillmore Street in Pacific Heights. The restaurant, expected to open in fall 2026, will offer both a la carte and tasting menus. Monami is part of venture capitalist Neil Mehta's Upper Fillmore Revitalization Project, which has involved purchasing multiple buildings along a three-block stretch of Fillmore Street. Mehta has also expressed interest in reopening the historic Clay Theatre as a high-end arthouse cinema.

moreNews.headline: Cow Hollow Home Sells for Nearly Double Asking Price, Signaling 'Mansion Shortage'
moreNews.body: A six-bedroom Cow Hollow home at 2512 Union Street sold for $15 million — nearly double its $7.95 million asking price — in what the San Francisco Standard called the biggest percentage over list price for a home above $5 million in 26 years. The all-cash sale closed in just two weeks. The transaction reflects an ultracompetitive luxury market across the Marina and Pacific Heights neighborhoods: in April 2026, 85% of San Francisco homes sold over list price, with the median sale going for a record 24.6% over asking, according to Compass data.

moreNews.headline: Presidio Main Post Construction Begins in July
moreNews.body: The Presidio Trust has announced that access and safety improvement construction around the Main Post area will run from July 2026 through Winter 2027. Visitors to the Presidio should expect noise, detours, and increased traffic in the area. The Trust advises planning ahead, allowing extra travel time, and following posted signage.
